trigger_keywords: what happens with quotes in text
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The description string is rendered using HTML entity encoding where double quotes become ", less than signs become < (wait, actually less than is <, greater than is > as per the table), so quotes are \" and less than is \<

trigger_keywords: how is an empty url resolved
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the JSON-LD step provides a null url, the final output resolves it to the literal string 'http://localhost'.

context_pointers.vague_reference: maintains the facade sequence for SEO
context_pointers.actual_meaning: This constraint forces the output order of metadata executions to be: Meta Title & Description, then Open Graph, then Twitter, then JSON-LD, defined by external constants and hardcoded render order.
This constraint ensures that a title or description input step is simultaneously propagated to the Meta channel, Open Graph properties, Twitter card properties, and JSON-LD name/description fields.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have more detail on that — check the codebase or the existing test cases for the exact behavior.

feature1_1_title.json.description: Title composition for the document head. The renderer always emits a <title> element plus the default description meta. A supplied page title is, by default, combined with the configured site/default title using a separator (page title first, then separator, then default). Callers may opt out of appending the default to render the page title verbatim, may override the separator, or may change the default title itself; when no explicit page title is given the default title is rendered alone.

cases.expected_output: <title>Kamehamehaaaaaaaa - It's Over 9000!</title><meta name="description" content="For those who helped create the Genki Dama">
<title>Kamehamehaaaaaaaa</title><meta name="description" content="For those who helped create the Genki Dama">
<title>Kamehamehaaaaaaaa | It's Over 9000!</title><meta name="description" content="For those who helped create the Genki Dama">
<title>Kamehamehaaaaaaaa</title><meta name="description" content="For those who helped create the Genki Dama">

feature1_2_description.json.description: Description meta handling. A supplied description is HTML-escaped (double quotes become ", < and > become entities) before being rendered into the description meta tag, protecting against markup injection while leaving non-ASCII letters intact. Passing a boolean false suppresses the description entirely so no description tag is emitted.

cases.expected_output: <title>It's Over 9000!</title><meta name="description" content="Kamehamehaaaaaaaa">
<title>It's Over 9000!</title><meta name="description" content=""Foo bar" -> abc">
<title>It's Over 9000!</title><meta name="description" content="de fidélisation des salariés">
<title>It's Over 9000!</title>

feature1_3_keywords.json.description: Keyword meta handling. Keywords may be supplied as a single comma-delimited string (rendered verbatim as the keywords meta content), or accumulated one at a time. When accumulating, a single keyword is appended to the running list, while a list of keywords is prepended ahead of the already-collected ones; the final keywords meta joins them with a comma-and-space separator.

cases.expected_output: <title>It's Over 9000!</title><meta name="description" content="For those who helped create the Genki Dama"><meta name="keywords" content="masenko,makankosappo">
<title>It's Over 9000!</title><meta name="description" content="For those who helped create the Genki Dama"><meta name="keywords" content="masenko, makankosappo">
<title>It's Over 9000!</title><meta name="description" content="For those who helped create the Genki Dama"><meta name="keywords" content="kienzan, tayoken, masenko, makankosappo">

feature1_4_custom_meta.json.description: Arbitrary custom meta tags. A custom tag is registered under a key together with a content value and the name of the attribute that should carry the key (defaulting to name). Tags are rendered after the standard title/description block. A previously added custom tag can be removed by its key, restoring the output to the baseline head.

cases.expected_output: <title>It's Over 9000!</title><meta name="description" content="For those who helped create the Genki Dama"><meta test="custom-meta" content="value">
<title>It's Over 9000!</title><meta name="description" content="For those who helped create the Genki Dama">

feature1_5_link_relations.json.description: Link relation tags for SEO and navigation. The renderer can emit canonical, AMP, pagination previous/next, and per-language alternate link elements. Each is rendered as a self-closing link element with the relation-specific rel attribute and the supplied URL; alternate-language links additionally carry the hreflang code.

cases.expected_output: <title>It's Over 9000!</title><meta name="description" content="For those who helped create the Genki Dama"><link rel="canonical" href="http://domain.com"/>
<title>It's Over 9000!</title><meta name="description" content="For those who helped create the Genki Dama"><link rel="amphtml" href="http://domain.com/amp"/>
<title>It's Over 9000!</title><meta name="description" content="For those who helped create the Genki Dama"><link rel="next" href="http://domain.com"/>
<title>It's Over 9000!</title><meta name="description" content="For those who helped create the Genki Dama"><link rel="prev" href="http://domain.com"/>
<title>It's Over 9000!</title><meta name="description" content="For those who helped create the Genki Dama"><link rel="alternate" hreflang="en" href="http://domain.com"/>

feature1_6_robots_reset.json.description: Robots directive and reset. A robots crawl directive is rendered as a robots meta tag. The reset operation clears all accumulated state (description override, keywords, pagination, canonical, custom tags, alternate languages, robots) and returns the output to the baseline title/description head.

cases.expected_output: <title>It's Over 9000!</title><meta name="description" content="For those who helped create the Genki Dama"><meta name="robots" content="all">
<title>It's Over 9000!</title><meta name="description" content="For those who helped create the Genki Dama">

feature1_7_notranslate_title.json.description: Optional no-translate styling on the title. When the document head is configured to mark the title as non-translatable, the rendered title element carries a notranslate class so automated page translators leave it untouched. The description meta is unaffected.

defaults.title: It's Over 9000!
defaults.description: For those who helped create the Genki Dama

cases.expected_output: <title class="notranslate">It's Over 9000!</title><meta name="description" content="For those who helped create the Genki Dama">

feature2_1_basic_properties.json.description: Open Graph property tags for social link previews. Title and description are rendered as og:title and og:description property meta tags; a supplied URL adds an og:url tag. When the configured defaults provide a title and description, they are emitted even if the caller only sets the URL. Each tag uses the property/content meta shape with the og: prefix.

steps.description: This is a test by Ali.

cases.expected_output: <meta property="og:title" content="Hello, Ali" /><meta property="og:description" content="This is a test by Ali." />

<meta property="og:title" content="Over 9000 Thousand!" /><meta property="og:description" content="For those who helped create the Genki Dama" /><meta property="og:url" content="https://www.domain.com" />

feature2_2_typed_object.json.description: Typed Open Graph objects with structured sub-properties. After declaring an object type (e.g. article), structured attributes for that type can be attached; a list-valued attribute expands into one property tag per element. The type tag is rendered first, followed by the default title/description, then the type-namespaced attribute tags (e.g. article:tag). Attributes are only accepted when they belong to the declared object type's allowed set.

cases.expected_output: <meta property="og:type" content="article" /><meta property="og:title" content="Over 9000 Thousand!" /><meta property="og:description" content="For those who helped create the Genki Dama" /><meta property="article:tag" content="Example" /><meta property="article:tag" content="tags" /><meta property="article:tag" content="test" />

feature3_1_card_fields.json.description: Twitter Card meta tags. Each card field maps to a name/content meta tag prefixed with twitter:. Title, site handle, URL, and card type are rendered verbatim; the description field is HTML-escaped before output to neutralize markup. Only the fields the caller sets are emitted (no defaults).

cases.expected_output: <meta name="twitter:title" content="Kamehamehaaaaaaaa" />
<meta name="twitter:site" content="http://kakaroto.9000" />
<meta name="twitter:url" content="http://kakaroto.9000" />
<meta name="twitter:card" content="sayajin" />
<meta name="twitter:description" content=""Foo bar" -> abc" />

feature3_2_card_images.json.description: Twitter Card image fields. A single image is rendered as one twitter:image tag. A list of images is rendered as a series of indexed tags (twitter:images0, twitter:images1, ...), one per supplied image, preserving order.

cases.expected_output: <meta name="twitter:image" content="sayajin.png" />
<meta name="twitter:images0" content="sayajin.png" /><meta name="twitter:images1" content="namekusei.png" />

feature4_1_core_fields.json.description: JSON-LD structured-data document. The renderer emits a single application/ld+json script element wrapping a JSON object that always starts with the schema.org context and a type, then a name (from title) and description. Callers may override the title, description, or type; defaults supply name and description when not overridden. Note slashes inside URLs/the context are escaped in the JSON output, and the description text is embedded literally (no HTML escaping at this layer).

cases.expected_output: <script type="application/ld+json">{"@context":"https:\/\/schema.org","@type":"WebPage","name":"Kamehamehaaaaaaaa","description":"For those who helped create the Genki Dama"}</script>
<script type="application/ld+json">{"@context":"https:\/\/schema.org","@type":"WebPage","name":"Over 9000 Thousand!","description":"Kamehamehaaaaaaaa"}</script>
<script type="application/ld+json">{"@context":"https:\/\/schema.org","@type":"sayajin","name":"Over 9000 Thousand!","description":"For those who helped create the Genki Dama"}</script>
<script type="application/ld+json">{"@context":"https:\/\/schema.org","@type":"WebPage","name":"Over 9000 Thousand!","description":"\"Foo bar\" -> abc"}</script>

feature4_2_url_resolution.json.description: JSON-LD url field resolution. Setting an explicit URL (whether via the dedicated url field or a site reference) appends a url entry to the structured-data object. Setting the URL to null resolves it to the current request URL, which in the default runtime environment is http://localhost.

cases.expected_output: <script type="application/ld+json">{"@context":"https:\/\/schema.org","@type":"WebPage","name":"Over 9000 Thousand!","description":"For those who helped create the Genki Dama","url":"http:\/\/kakaroto.9000"}</script>

<script type="application/ld+json">{"@context":"https:\/\/schema.org","@type":"WebPage","name":"Over 9000 Thousand!","description":"For those who helped create the Genki Dama","url":"http:\/\/localhost"}</script>

feature4_3_images.json.description: JSON-LD image field. A single image is serialized as a scalar image string. A list of images is serialized as a JSON array under the image key, preserving order. The image entry appears after the core name/description fields.

cases.expected_output: <script type="application/ld+json">{"@context":"https:\/\/schema.org","@type":"WebPage","name":"Over 9000 Thousand!","description":"For those who helped create the Genki Dama","image":"sayajin.png"}</script>

<script type="application/ld+json">{"@context":"https:\/\/schema.org","@type":"WebPage","name":"Over 9000 Thousand!","description":"For those who helped create the Genki Dama","image":["sayajin.png","namekusei.png"]}</script>

feature4_4_custom_values.json.description: Arbitrary custom JSON-LD properties. Callers can attach extra key/value pairs to the structured-data object, either one at a time or in bulk. Scalar values render as JSON scalars; object values render as nested JSON objects. Custom entries appear after the core name/description fields in insertion order.

cases.expected_output: <script type="application/ld+json">{"@context":"https:\/\/schema.org","@type":"WebPage","name":"Over 9000 Thousand!","description":"For those who helped create the Genki Dama","test":"1-2-3","another":"test-value"}</script>

<script type="application/ld+json">{"@context":"https:\/\/schema.org","@type":"WebPage","name":"Over 9000 Thousand!","description":"For those who helped create the Genki Dama","author":{"@type":"Organization","name":"SeoTools","url":"https:\/\/github.com\/artesaos\/seotools"}}</script>

<script type="application/ld+json">{"@context":"https:\/\/schema.org","@type":"WebPage","name":"Over 9000 Thousand!","description":"For those who helped create the Genki Dama","test":"1-2-3","author":{"@type":"Organization","name":"SeoTools"}}</script>

feature5_multi_graph.json.description: Multiple JSON-LD structured-data graphs in one document. A collection holds several independent graphs; each graph is an ordered list of field operations (title/description/etc.) applied to its own structured-data object. The renderer emits a script element per graph, concatenated in order, but only when the collection holds more than one graph — a collection with a single graph produces no output. Field operations target the graph they belong to, so per-graph overrides (e.g. a distinct title on the second graph) appear only in that graph's script.

cases.expected_output: <script type="application/ld+json">{"@context":"https:\/\/schema.org","@type":"WebPage","name":"Over 9000 Thousand!","description":"For those who helped create the Genki Dama"}</script><script type="application/ld+json">{"@context":"https:\/\/schema.org","@type":"WebPage","name":"Kamehamehaaaaaaaa","description":"For those who helped create the Genki Dama"}</script>

<script type="application/ld+json">{"@context":"https:\/\/schema.org","@type":"WebPage","name":"Over 9000 Thousand!","description":"For those who helped create the Genki Dama"}</script><script type="application/ld+json">{"@context":"https:\/\/schema.org","@type":"WebPage","name":"Over 9000 Thousand!","description":"For those who helped create the Genki Dama"}</script><script type="application/ld+json">{"@context":"https:\/\/schema.org","@type":"WebPage","name":"Over 9000 Thousand!","description":"For those who helped create the Genki Dama"}</script>

feature6_unified_facade.json.description: Unified facade that fans a single high-level instruction out to all markup channels at once and renders their combined output in a fixed order: standard head tags, then Open Graph, then Twitter, then JSON-LD. Setting the title updates the head title (composed with the default), the og:title, twitter:title, and the JSON-LD name simultaneously. Setting the description updates the head description meta, og:description, twitter:description, and the JSON-LD description. With no instructions, the defaults for each channel are rendered.

cases.expected_output: <title>It's Over 9000!</title><meta name="description" content="For those who helped create the Genki Dama"><meta property="og:title" content="Over 9000 Thousand!" /><meta property="og:description" content="For those who helped create the Genki Dama" /><script type="application/ld+json">{"@context":"https:\/\/schema.org","@type":"WebPage","name":"Over 9000 Thousand!","description":"For those who helped create the Genki Dama"}</script>

<title>Kamehamehaaaaaaa - It's Over 9000!</title><meta name="description" content="For those who helped create the Genki Dama"><meta property="og:title" content="Kamehamehaaaaaaa" /><meta property="og:description" content="For those who helped create the Genki Dama" /><meta name="twitter:title" content="Kamehamehaaaaaaa" /><script type="application/ld+json">{"@context":"https:\/\/schema.org","@type":"WebPage","name":"Kamehamehaaaaaaa","description":"For those who helped create the Genki Dama"}</script>

<title>It's Over 9000!</title><meta name="description" content="Kamehamehaaaaaaa"><meta property="og:description" content="Kamehamehaaaaaaa" /><meta property="og:title" content="Over 9000 Thousand!" /><meta name="twitter:description" content="Kamehamehaaaaaaa" /><script type="application/ld+json">{"@context":"https:\/\/schema.org","@type":"WebPage","name":"Over 9000 Thousand!","description":"Kamehamehaaaaaaa"}</script>

<title>It's Over 9000!</title><meta name="description" content="For those who helped create the Genki Dama"><link rel="canonical" href="http://domain.com"/><meta property="og:title" content="Over 9000 Thousand!" /><meta property="og:description" content="For those who helped create the Genki Dama" /><script type="application/ld+json">{"@context":"https:\/\/schema.org","@type":"WebPage","name":"Over 9000 Thousand!","description":"For those who helped create the Genki Dama"}</script>

<title>It's Over 9000!</title><meta name="description" content="For those who helped create the Genki Dama"><meta property="og:title" content="Over 9000 Thousand!" /><meta property="og:description" content="For those who helped create the Genki Dama" /><meta property="og:image" content="Kamehamehaaaaaaa.png" /><meta property="og:image" content="Kamehamehaaaaaaa.png" /><meta name="twitter:image" content="Kamehamehaaaaaaa.png" /><script type="application/ld+json">{"@context":"https:\/\/schema.org","@type":"WebPage","name":"Over 9000 Thousand!","description":"For those who helped create the Genki Dama","image":["Kamehamehaaaaaaa.png","Kamehamehaaaaaaa.png"]}</script>

_constraints.output_rule: Title rendered as [page_title] + separator + [default_title]

_constraints.input_condition: channel == 'meta' AND step with 'title' containing {'text': ..., 'append_default': false}
_constraints.output_rule: Title rendered ONLY as ['text' value]
Title rendered as [page_title] + ['title_separator' value] + [default_title]
Default title replaced by ['config.defaults.title' value]

_constraints.input_condition: channel == 'meta' AND step with 'description' containing string
_constraints.output_rule: Description rendered with HTML entities escaped (" -> ", < -> <, > -> >)

_constraints.input_condition: channel == 'meta' AND step with 'description' is boolean false
_constraints.output_rule: No description meta tag is emitted

_constraints.input_condition: channel == 'meta' AND step with keyword field containing string
_constraints.output_rule: String keyword appended to running list; final output joined with ', ' (comma-space)

_constraints.input_condition: channel == 'meta' AND step with keyword field containing array
_constraints.output_rule: Array elements prepended to running list in order; final output joined with ', ' (comma-space)
Tag rendered as `<meta [attribute]="name" content="content">`
Target custom tag removed; state reverts to baseline before modification
Meta tag rendered as `<meta name="robots" content="robots_value">`
All accumulated state cleared; output shows only baseline title and default description meta
Title tag rendered with class='notranslate'

_constraints.input_condition: channel == 'opengraph' AND step with 'title' or 'description' or 'url'
_constraints.output_rule: Property tags rendered as `<meta property="og:['field']" content="value">`

_constraints.input_condition: channel == 'opengraph' AND NO 'title' or 'description' step provided
_constraints.output_rule: Default OG title and description are emitted
First `og:type`, then default title/desc, then `schema:type:[field_name]` tags for each array element

_constraints.input_condition: channel == 'twitter' AND step with any card field
_constraints.output_rule: Property tag rendered as `<meta name="twitter:['field']" content="value">`
Tag name rendered as `twitter:images[INDEX]` where INDEX starts at 0

_constraints.input_condition: channel == 'jsonld' AND step with 'title' or 'description' or 'type'
_constraints.output_rule: JSON-LD script structure: `@context: schema.org`, `@type: value`, `name`, `description`

_constraints.input_condition: channel == 'jsonld' AND step with 'site' OR 'url' (not null)
_constraints.output_rule: URL field added as `"url":"http://site_value"`
URL field resolved to hardcoded `http://localhost`
Single image: `"image":"path"`; Array: `"image":["path",...]`
Nested object implies `"key": {nested: {}}` in output JSON; nested keys escaped properly
No output emitted
Emitted in fixed order: Title/Description -> OG -> Twitter -> JSON-LD
Value propagated to: Meta Title, OG Title/Desc, Twitter Title/Desc, JSON-LD Name/Desc simultaneously

_constraints.input_condition: Global Constraint for all channels and facets
_constraints.output_rule: JSON input -> Minified string output (no whitespace, tags concatenated)

vague_refs.vague_phrase: maintains the facade sequence for SEO
vague_refs.actual_meaning: This constraint forces the output order of metadata executions to be: Meta Title & Description, then Open Graph, then Twitter, then JSON-LD, defined by external constants and hardcoded render order.
This constraint ensures that a title or description input step is simultaneously propagated to the Meta channel, Open Graph properties, Twitter card properties, and JSON-LD name/description fields.
